items.summary: Clindamycin Phosphate Injection (IM) is in active FDA shortage due to demand increase; this drug is non-formulary at this hospital with 79 active orders in 30 days from Ambulatory, warranting monitoring despite non-formulary status.
items.rationale: Rule W2 applies: today_status=Current, formulary_status=non-formulary, active_orders_30d=79 (>0). The drug is non-formulary and restricted to Oncology-only per formulary, yet 79 orders were placed in the last 30 days from Ambulatory — this volume warrants attention. No preferred formulary alternatives are listed. RxNorm alternatives are all class-members only (confidence ceiling: max medium). The top-1 alternative (sulfacetamide) is a topical acne product with no clinical relevance as an injectable antibiotic substitute; it is not a viable recommendation. Chloramphenicol (RxCUI 2348) has no active shortage record but carries significant toxicity concerns not reflected in retrieved label data for this briefing. No clinically equivalent injectable alternative with verified shortage-free status and label support can be confirmed from the retrieved data; alternatives confidence is low.

alternatives.name: clindamycin (oral formulation)
alternatives.rationale: Oral clindamycin shares the same active moiety; step-down from IM to oral is clinically supported per the FDA label ('Parenteral therapy may be changed to oral CLEOCIN PEDIATRIC or CLEOCIN HCl Capsules when the condition warrants'). Feasibility depends on patient's ability to tolerate oral therapy. Shortage status for oral clindamycin unverified in this briefing run.

alternatives.name: chloramphenicol
alternatives.rationale: Class-member antibiotic with anaerobic and gram-positive coverage overlapping clindamycin indications. No active shortage record found. However, chloramphenicol carries serious toxicity (aplastic anemia, gray baby syndrome) and is not a routine substitute; clinical pharmacist review required before use. Shortage status unverified beyond top-2 check. Label data not retrieved in this briefing.

alternatives.name: erythromycin
alternatives.rationale: FDA label for clindamycin explicitly references erythromycin as a 'less toxic alternative' to consider before selecting clindamycin. Spectrum differs (limited anaerobic coverage); clinical appropriateness depends on indication. Shortage status unverified (ranked 3+).

citations.claim: Clindamycin Phosphate Injection shortage status is Current as of 04/22/2026; reason: demand increase; availability: limited.

Clindamycin Phosphate Injection is indicated for serious infections caused by susceptible anaerobes, streptococci, pneumococci, and staphylococci; erythromycin cited as less toxic alternative; oral step-down supported when condition warrants.
Clindamycin Phosphate IM/IV dosing: 600–2700 mg/day in adults; oral step-down to clindamycin palmitate or HCl permitted when clinically appropriate.
Sulfacetamide (top-1 RxNorm alternative) label indicates topical use for acne/rosacea only — not clinically equivalent to injectable clindamycin; rejected as alternative.

items.recommended_action: Verify whether the 79 Ambulatory orders represent appropriate non-formulary use under Oncology restriction or a compliance gap; contact procurement to assess clindamycin injection inventory levels, and evaluate oral clindamycin step-down eligibility for affected patients.

items.summary: Amino Acid Injection (IV parenteral nutrition component) is in active shortage with 77 orders in the last 30 days in Pediatrics and no formulary alternatives available.
items.rationale: Rule C2 applies: today_status is Current, route is IV, and Pediatrics is an at-risk department for a drug with no preferred alternatives available. Rule C1 also independently applies: preferred formulary status, 77 active orders (>20), and zero available preferred alternatives not in shortage. Both rules converge on Critical. The RxNorm therapeutic alternatives returned (trazodone, vilazodone, tryptophan, etc.) are antidepressants/serotonergic agents — pharmacologically unrelated to IV amino acids for parenteral nutrition — indicating a class-mapping error; these are not clinically acceptable substitutes. No genuine therapeutic alternative exists in this dataset. Confidence is capped at medium due to unknown shortage reason (FDA record provides no shortage_reason) and because the alternative list is pharmacologically invalid, leaving shortage status of any true substitute unverified.

alternatives.name: trazodone
alternatives.rationale: REJECTED — RxNorm returned this as a class-member alternative, but trazodone is an oral antidepressant indicated for major depressive disorder (per openFDA label). It has no clinical equivalence to IV amino acid solutions used for parenteral nutrition. This alternative is not acceptable.

alternatives.name: vilazodone
alternatives.rationale: REJECTED — Class-member only; vilazodone is an antidepressant with no clinical relationship to IV amino acids for parenteral nutrition. Not an acceptable substitute.

citations.claim: Amino Acid Injection (RxCUI 801133) shortage status is Current as of 04/20/2026

PROSOL (Amino Acid Injection) is indicated as a source of amino acids for parenteral nutrition when oral/enteral nutrition is not possible; pediatric dosing is explicitly defined by age group including neonates and infants
PROSOL does not contain cysteine and taurine, conditionally essential amino acids for neonates and infants — relevant to Pediatrics department impact
Trazodone is indicated for major depressive disorder in adults only — confirms it is not a clinical substitute for IV amino acid injection

items.recommended_action: Escalate immediately to P&T and pharmacy leadership: contact alternate suppliers of IV amino acid solutions (e.g., Clinisol, Travasol, FreAmine) for emergency procurement, prioritize allocation to Pediatrics/NICU patients, and evaluate whether any patients can transition to enteral nutrition to conserve supply.

items.summary: Dextrose 5% Injection (D5W) is currently in active FDA shortage; this hospital's non-formulary IV formulation has 77 active orders in Pediatrics over the last 30 days, creating meaningful supply risk for a high-volume pediatric fluid.
items.rationale: Rule W2 applies: today_status=Current, formulary_status=non-formulary, active_orders_30d=77 (>0). Rule C2 does not trigger because the affected department is Pediatrics, not ICU/ED/Oncology/Surgery/Anesthesia. Rule C1 does not apply because formulary_status is non-formulary (not preferred). Rule W1 does not apply because no formulary-listed preferred alternatives exist. Rule W2 is the first matching rule, yielding Watch. Active order volume (77) is notably high for a non-formulary item and warrants urgent formulary evaluation. Confidence ceiling applied: all retrieved therapeutic alternatives are class-members only (no clinical equivalence proof per label data review), capping confidence at medium. Additionally, the top-1 alternative label data returned is clearly a consumer OTC anti-nausea product (not a parenteral glucose product), meaning label data is clinically unusable for equivalence verification, further reinforcing medium ceiling.

alternatives.name: glucose
alternatives.rationale: Glucose (dextrose) is the same active moiety; a parenteral glucose/dextrose formulation from an unaffected supplier or different concentration may serve as a functional substitute for IV hydration and caloric support per the class-member mapping. No FDA shortage record found for RxCUI 4850. Clinical equivalence confirmation requires pharmacist review of concentration, osmolality, and pediatric dosing suitability.

alternatives.name: glycine
alternatives.rationale: Glycine is listed as a class-member alternative by RxNorm; however, glycine parenteral solutions are not clinically equivalent to D5W for general IV hydration or caloric support and are indicated primarily as irrigation solutions. This alternative is not appropriate for substitution without specialist pharmacist and physician review. No FDA shortage record found for RxCUI 4919. Shortage status unverified for RxCUI 6628 (mannitol) and RxCUI 9945 (sorbitol).

citations.claim: Dextrose Monohydrate 5% Injection RxCUI 1795477 status is Current as of 04/20/2026

Dextrose 5% Injection is indicated for use in adults and pediatric patients as a source of calories and water for hydration; contraindicated in hypersensitivity to corn products; contains aluminum with toxicity risk in premature neonates and renally-impaired patients

items.recommended_action: Notify Pediatrics and pharmacy leadership of the active D5W shortage; identify an alternative supplier or equivalent parenteral dextrose formulation not affected by the shortage, and initiate P&T review to formally address formulary status given 77 active orders in the past 30 days.

items.summary: Clonazepam Tablet (non-preferred, oral) entered active shortage as of 01/05/2026 with 76 orders in the past 30 days across ER, Pediatrics, and Oncology; no formulary alternatives are designated, and the only RxNorm-returned alternative is the same drug (clonazepam, RxCUI 2598), leaving no distinct substitution path.
items.rationale: Rule W2 applies: today_status=Current, formulary_status=non-preferred, active_orders_30d=76 (>0). Rule W1 does not apply because no clinically distinct alternative is available — the sole RxNorm therapeutic alternative returned (RxCUI 2598) is clonazepam itself, not a separate drug. Rules C1/C2 do not apply because formulary_status is non-preferred and preferred_alternatives minus alts_in_shortage is vacuously empty without a high-volume preferred designation. Rule C3 does not apply because yesterday_status was absent (not a re-occurrence from Resolved). Abrupt discontinuation risk is clinically significant — the FDA label explicitly warns that abrupt reduction can precipitate life-threatening seizures and status epilepticus, elevating operational urgency even under Watch. Confidence is capped at medium: the only alternative is a class-member match (same drug, no distinct substitute), and yesterday_status is absent (non-canonical input).

alternatives.name: clonazepam
alternatives.rationale: RxNorm returned clonazepam (RxCUI 2598) as the sole therapeutic alternative; this is the same active ingredient as the shortage drug and does not represent a distinct substitute. No shortage record exists for RxCUI 2598, but this alternative provides no clinical differentiation. Clinicians should evaluate other benzodiazepine class members (e.g., diazepam, lorazepam) or indication-specific anticonvulsants (e.g., valproate, lamotrigine for seizure indications) via P&T review; those agents were not returned by the RxNorm tool and cannot be cited here.

citations.claim: Clonazepam Tablet shortage status is Current as of update date 01/05/2026; RxCUIs 197527, 197528, 197529 confirmed in record.
Clonazepam tablets indicated for seizure disorders (Lennox-Gastaut syndrome, akinetic/myoclonic seizures, absence seizures) and panic disorder per FDA-approved labeling.
FDA label warns that abrupt discontinuation or rapid dosage reduction may precipitate acute withdrawal reactions including life-threatening seizures and status epilepticus.
Pediatric dosing established in label (0.01–0.03 mg/kg/day for infants/children up to 10 years or 30 kg); relevant given Pediatrics department usage.
Top-1 alternative (RxCUI 2598, clonazepam) label confirms identical indication profile and safety warnings to the shortage drug — no clinical differentiation.
RxNorm therapeutic alternatives query for RxCUI 197527 returned only clonazepam (RxCUI 2598) as a class-member alternative.

items.recommended_action: Identify all active clonazepam orders across ER, Pediatrics, and Oncology; do not abruptly discontinue any patient — taper per label guidance — and escalate to P&T for emergency formulary review of a clinically distinct benzodiazepine or indication-appropriate anticonvulsant substitute, as no distinct RxNorm alternative was returned.

items.summary: Methylprednisolone Acetate Injection (DEPO-MEDROL) is in active shortage as of 04/22/2026; this restricted formulary drug has 74 active orders in 30 days across Surgery, Ambulatory, and ICU with no preferred alternatives on formulary.
items.rationale: Rule C2 applies: today_status is Current, route is INTRALESIONAL (injected corticosteroid administered in procedural settings across ICU and Surgery), and no preferred alternatives exist on formulary after shortage cross-check. Active orders of 74 in 30 days further confirms high utilization. Yesterday's status was absent from snapshot (new entry), which aligns with diff bucket 'new' — Rule C3 does not directly apply as yesterday_status is absent rather than 'Resolved', but C2 is satisfied independently. Confidence is medium rather than high because the top-1 alternative label data retrieved (hydrocortisone) reflects an OTC topical product, not an injectable formulation equivalent, reducing clinical equivalence certainty for intralesional use; this imposes the class-member-only ceiling of max medium.

alternatives.name: hydrocortisone
alternatives.rationale: Hydrocortisone is a corticosteroid class member indicated for inflammatory dermatologic conditions; however, the label data returned reflects an OTC topical formulation, not an injectable preparation suitable for intralesional administration. Injectable hydrocortisone acetate exists clinically but was not confirmed by label data in this retrieval. Clinical pharmacist must verify injectable formulation availability and dosing equivalence before substitution. No shortage record found for RxCUI 5492.

alternatives.name: methylprednisolone
alternatives.rationale: Methylprednisolone (soluble formulations, e.g., methylprednisolone sodium succinate) is the same parent molecule but a different salt/formulation; may not be interchangeable with the acetate depot form for intralesional use. No shortage record found for RxCUI 6902. Pharmacist must confirm whether a non-acetate formulation meets clinical requirements for intralesional indication.

alternatives.name: prednisolone
alternatives.rationale: Prednisolone is a corticosteroid class member. Shortage status unverified (only top-2 alternatives checked per protocol). Injectable prednisolone acetate exists for intralesional use but formulary status and supply availability at this institution are unknown. Clinical pharmacist must verify formulation, route compatibility, and supply.

citations.claim: Methylprednisolone Acetate Injection status is Current as of 04/22/2026; RxCUI list includes 1358510.

DEPO-MEDROL is indicated for intralesional administration in alopecia areata, discoid lupus erythematosus, keloids, granuloma annulare, lichen planus, lichen simplex chronicus, psoriatic plaques, and necrobiosis lipoidica diabeticorum; warnings include immunosuppression, infection risk, and benzyl alcohol toxicity.
Hydrocortisone label retrieved reflects OTC topical formulation only; injectable intralesional equivalence not confirmed by this label record.
Therapeutic alternatives hydrocortisone (5492), methylprednisolone (6902), and prednisolone (8638) identified as corticosteroid class members via RxNorm.

items.recommended_action: Escalate to P&T committee for emergency formulary review of injectable corticosteroid alternatives (triamcinolone acetonide injection or hydrocortisone acetate injection) for intralesional use; contact current suppliers for allocation status and implement conservation protocol for the 74 active orders across Surgery, Ambulatory, and ICU.
